---
title: "Conway's Blobs"
jupyter: brainvomit
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

> _Scaffold — prose and interpretation to be filled in. The rules and base cases
> below are defined from the design docs; charts and animations are from a real run._

## What it is

`blobsim` is a small world that runs on a grid. Each cell can hold one **blob** — a
simple creature with an energy level. Step by step, every blob senses its
neighbours, picks an action (move, eat, reproduce, or rest), and pays for it in
energy. Blobs that run out of energy die; well-fed ones reproduce. The grid itself
holds energy that blobs eat, and that may or may not replenish over time.

<!-- TODO: what question this world is meant to explore. -->

## How it is designed

The grid wraps around at the edges (a blob walking off the right side reappears on
the left). Every step runs the same fixed sequence:

> observe → decide → resolve who-goes-where → charge energy → act → absorb from the
> grid → update the environment → remove the dead → record

The behaviour of a blob ("rules"), the behaviour of the environment (decaying vs.
replenishing), and how movement conflicts are settled are each swappable without
touching the rest.

<!-- TODO: more on the sensing model and the action costs, in your words. -->

## Inspiration

It starts from Conway's *Game of Life* — cells on a grid that live or die by their
neighbours — and asks what happens when each cell becomes an individual that has to
*earn its living*: carry energy, spend it to act, and starve if it can't. The result
sits somewhere between a cellular automaton and a tiny ecology.

<!-- TODO: the personal hook — why this was worth building. -->

## The rules it must obey

Five properties hold at every step, by construction. They are what makes the world
predictable enough to reason about:

- **Energy is conserved.** At any moment, the energy held by living blobs, plus the
  energy left in the grid, plus everything spent so far, equals what the world
  started with (plus anything the environment has since added). A running tally
  re-checks this equality every single step, so a bug that quietly leaks or invents
  energy cannot hide.
- **It is deterministic.** The same starting seed reproduces the exact same run,
  step for step.
- **One blob per cell.** No two blobs ever occupy the same square.
- **Reproduction only moves energy.** A new blob is funded entirely out of its
  parent's energy; splitting never creates any.
- **Death has a hard floor.** A blob whose energy falls to the death threshold is
  removed immediately; every surviving blob sits strictly above it.

## See it run

A finite grid with no replenishment can only end one way — the blobs eat through the
available energy and die out:

![Two kinds of blob draw down a finite grid until none are left.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc1_decay_extinction.gif)

One run's population and total energy over time:

```{python}
#| label: setup
import pandas as pd
import plotly.io as pio
import plotly.graph_objects as go
pio.templates.default = "plotly"

import blobsim
```

```{python}
#| label: live-sim
from blobsim import (
    Simulation, SimulationConfig, SpeciesConfig, BlobAttributes,
    RegeneratingEnvironment, ActionType, SensingLevel, MOORE,
)
from blobsim.species import GrazerRules

attrs = BlobAttributes.create(
    species="grazer", max_energy=10.0, offspring_energy=3.0,
    reproduction_threshold=7.0, base_metabolic_cost=0.1, move_cost=0.2,
    reproduce_cost=0.5, observation_radius=1, sensing_level=SensingLevel.BASIC,
    allowed_actions=frozenset(ActionType), allowed_directions=MOORE,
)
config = SimulationConfig(
    grid_size=30,
    environment=RegeneratingEnvironment(rate=0.05, capacity=2.0, initial_cell_energy=1.0),
    species=[SpeciesConfig(rules_factory=GrazerRules, attributes=attrs, count=25, initial_energy=5.0)],
    seed=42,
)
sim = Simulation(config)
traj = pd.DataFrame([
    {"step": s.step, "population": s.n_alive, "total_energy": s.e_blobs + s.e_grid}
    for s in sim.iterate(steps=200)
])

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_scatter(x=traj.step, y=traj.population, name="population", yaxis="y1")
fig.add_scatter(x=traj.step, y=traj.total_energy, name="total energy (blobs + grid)", yaxis="y2")
fig.update_layout(
    height=380, margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=10, b=0), xaxis_title="step",
    yaxis=dict(title="population"),
    yaxis2=dict(title="energy", overlaying="y", side="right", showgrid=False),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=1.12),
)
fig
```

<!-- TODO: read off the population dynamics. -->

## Benchmarking is a precondition

Everything in this section is **validation, not the end goal**. Before trusting the
simulation on complicated scenarios — where the right answer isn't known in advance —
it has to first reproduce the cases where the answer *is* known. Each base case below
computes a target *analytically from the rules* (a conservation identity, an expected
lifespan, a stable population) and then checks the simulation lands on it within
tolerance. Passing them all is a statistical precondition: evidence the engine is
predictable and internally consistent, so that later, non-base-case results can be
read as findings rather than bugs.

## The base cases

### A single blob's lifespan

**The rule.** With a positive move cost, a lone blob lives in two phases: while it
has enough energy it wanders (paying a higher average cost per step), and once it
drops below that level it can only rest at a fixed cost until it starves. Summing the
two phases predicts an average lifespan of about **186 steps**; the run observed
185.9.

<!-- TODO: why the two-phase split, in your words. -->

### Guaranteed extinction on a finite grid

**The rule.** If the environment adds no energy, then every step at least some energy
is spent and never returned, so the world's total can only fall. With a finite
starting amount, every blob must therefore die within a bounded number of steps — and
reproduction can't save them, because offspring are funded from parents (energy only
moves, it isn't created). The animation above shows exactly this.

<!-- TODO. -->

### Boom, then bust

**The rule.** Give blobs a rich grid and they multiply fast — but the peak is capped
by *space*, not energy: with one blob per cell, the population can't exceed the number
of cells. Once it fills the grid and the food is drawn down, it collapses to
extinction.

![A population explodes to fill the grid, then crashes.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc4_boom_bust.gif)

<!-- TODO: the overshoot → depletion → die-off mechanism. -->

### A carrying capacity

**The rule.** When the grid replenishes, the population settles where energy coming in
balances energy spent. Equating the two gives a predicted stable size

$$N^* \approx \frac{\text{rate}\times(\text{cells})}{\text{rate} + \text{avg cost per blob}} \approx 106.$$

Starting from very crowded and very sparse, both runs converge to roughly the same
level — evidence it's a genuine attractor, not an artifact of where you start.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Sparse start — grows toward the steady level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_sparse.gif)

![Crowded start — relaxes down to the same level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_dense.gif)
:::

<!-- TODO: the surprise — observed settles ~25% above the prediction, because the
mean-field math assumes blobs are well-mixed; real blobs cluster and spread out, so
fewer of their moves collide and get wasted. -->

### A lone blob's energy

**The rule.** A single blob on a fast-replenishing grid absorbs more each step than it
spends, so its energy climbs quickly to the maximum it can hold and then stays pinned
there — steady, with essentially no variation.

<!-- TODO. -->

### The extinction threshold

**The rule.** Whether a population survives hinges on how fast the grid replenishes.
There is a **critical rate**: below it, each blob can't absorb enough to replace what
it spends, so on average it leaves fewer than one surviving offspring and the line
dies out; above it, survival becomes possible. The crossover is sharp — a phase
transition.

::: {layout-ncol=2}
![Below the critical rate — the population dies out.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_extinction.gif)

![Above the critical rate — the population survives.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_survival.gif)
:::

<!-- TODO. -->

### Grazers beat walkers

**The rule.** Put two strategies on one grid: undirected **random walkers** and
**grazers** that move toward the richest neighbouring cell. With identical
metabolism, the grazers absorb more per step, so they reproduce faster and eventually
take over. Greater efficiency compounds into dominance.

![Energy-seeking grazers outcompete random walkers.](/assets/images/blobsim/c3_competition.gif)

<!-- TODO: what decides the margin / how fast displacement happens. -->

## What this unlocks

With the base cases reproduced and energy conserved throughout, the engine is
trustworthy on scenarios that *can't* be solved by hand — multi-species mixes, richer
environments, new behaviours — where a surprising result is a finding about the model,
not a bug in it.

<!-- TODO: which complex scenarios you actually want to explore next. -->

## What I took away, in order

<!-- TODO: ordered takeaways, e.g.
1. Conservation is the backbone — every base case is ultimately energy bookkeeping.
2. On a finite grid, extinction is guaranteed; reproduction only moves energy around.
3. Crowding (one-per-cell), not total energy, caps the population at its peak.
4. Survival has a sharp threshold in the replenishment rate.
5. The simplest population prediction runs ~25% low because it over-counts collisions.
-->
